front: Poem: dramatic monologues from perspectives of famous men's female counterparts including Mrs. Tiresias, Anne Hathaway, and Queen Herod
Poem: Shakespeare's wife holds "him in the casket of my widow's head"; marriage bed likened to "seas where he would dive for pearls"
back: "Anne Hathaway" from The World's Wife (Carol Ann Duffy)

front: Poem: opens the collection; speaker lets a wolf spot her for the sake of "poetry" and later kills the wolf with an axe; "sweet sixteen, never been, babe"
back: "Little Red-Cap" from The World's Wife (Carol Ann Duffy)

front: Poem: a nun thinks of her convent as "a land of grace"; references Heartbreak Hotel; prays for "the immortal soul of rock 'n' roll"
back: "Elvis's Twin Sister" from The World's Wife (Carol Ann Duffy)

front: Poem: speaker offers lover an onion instead of "a red rose or satin heart"; described as "a moon wrapped in brown paper"; "Its platinum loops shrink to a wedding ring / if you like"
work: Standing Female Nude and Other Poems

front: Poem: censored in schools for knife crime references; begins "Today I am going to kill something. Anything."; speaker kills a fly and a goldfish
work: Standing Female Nude and Other Poems

front: Poem: written from perspective of an artist's model; narrator imagines "the bourgeoisie will coo"; ends "It does not look like me"
work: Standing Female Nude and Other Poems

front: Poem: inverts Wilfred Owen's "Dulce et Decorum Est"; eulogizes WWI veterans
work: Standing Female Nude and Other Poems

front: Poem: inspired by Great Expectations; opens "Beloved sweetheart bastard"; ends "Don't think it's only the heart that b-b-b-breaks"; "Love's hate behind a white veil"
Play: 2015 National Theatre adaptation of a medieval morality play; features a birthday party with the rap "What's God Like? You're God Like"
Poem: husband "went out for his walk a man / and came home female"
back: "Mrs. Tiresias" from The World's Wife (Carol Ann Duffy)

front: Poem: wife declares "I keep his secret still- / the clever, cunning, callous bastard / didn't have a soul to sell"
back: "Mrs. Faust" from The World's Wife (Carol Ann Duffy)

front: Poem: husband loses interest; she "sawed and pulled and hacked / I wanted silence back"; campanologist involved
back: "Mrs. Quasimodo" from The World's Wife (Carol Ann Duffy)

front: Poem: written in response to the censorship of 'Education for Leisure'; references Romeo and Juliet's "star-cross'd lovers" and "Tybalt's death"
work: Standing Female Nude and Other Poems

front: Poem: from Standing Female Nude collection; begins "I have turned the newspaper boy into a diver for pearls"
work: Standing Female Nude and Other Poems

front: Poem: the snake-haired figure describes turning a bee into "a dull grey pebble" with her gaze
back: "Medusa" from The World's Wife (Carol Ann Duffy)
